name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِأَهْلِهِۦٓ إِنِّىٓ ءَانَسْتُ نَارًا سَـَٔاتِيكُم مِّنْهَا بِخَبَرٍ أَوْ ءَاتِيكُم بِشِهَابٍ قَبَسٍ لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَصْطَلُونَ (٧)
```

```english
When Moses said to his family, ‘Indeed I descry a fire! I will bring you some news from it, or bring you a firebrand so that you may warm yourselves.’ (7)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهَا نُودِىَ أَنۢ بُورِكَ مَن فِى ٱلنَّارِ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهَا وَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨)
```

```english
When he came to it, he was called: ‘Blessed is He who is in the fire and who is [as well] around it, and immaculate is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds!’ (8)
```

```arabic
يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّهُۥٓ أَنَا ٱللَّهُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٩)
```

```english
‘O Moses! Indeed I am Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise.’ (9)
```

```arabic
وَأَلْقِ عَصَاكَ ۚ فَلَمَّا رَءَاهَا تَهْتَزُّ كَأَنَّهَا جَآنٌّ وَلَّىٰ مُدْبِرًا وَلَمْ يُعَقِّبْ ۚ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ لَا تَخَفْ إِنِّى لَا يَخَافُ لَدَىَّ ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ (١٠)
```

```english
‘Throw down your staff!’ When he saw it wriggling, as if it were a snake, he turned his back [to flee], without looking back. ‘O Moses! ‘Do not be afraid. Indeed the apostles are not afraid before Me, (10)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا مَن ظَلَمَ ثُمَّ بَدَّلَ حُسْنًۢا بَعْدَ سُوٓءٍ فَإِنِّى غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١١)
```

```english
nor those who do wrong and then make up for [their] fault with goodness, for indeed I am all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (11)
```

```arabic
وَأَدْخِلْ يَدَكَ فِى جَيْبِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضَآءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوٓءٍ ۖ فِى تِسْعِ ءَايَـٰتٍ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَقَوْمِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ (١٢)
```

```english
‘Insert your hand into your shirt. It will emerge white and bright, without any fault—among nine signs meant for Pharaoh and his people. Indeed they are a transgressing lot.’ (12)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا جَآءَتْهُمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا مُبْصِرَةً قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٣)
```

```english
But when Our signs came to them, as eye-openers, they said, ‘This is plain magic.’ (13)
```

```arabic
وَجَحَدُوا۟ بِهَا وَٱسْتَيْقَنَتْهَآ أَنفُسُهُمْ ظُلْمًا وَعُلُوًّا ۚ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (١٤)
```

```english
They impugned them, wrongfully and out of arrogance, though they were convinced in their hearts [of their veracity]. So observe how the fate of the agents of corruption was! (14)
```

## (Commentary)

This is the first of the five stories alluded to in this Surah as a testimony to the glad tidings, warnings, promises, and threats mentioned at its beginning. In the first three stories—those of Musa, Dawud, and Sulayman—the aspect of promise (الوعد) prevails over the threat (الوعيد), while in the last two, the opposite is the case.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِذْ قالَ مُوسى لِأَهْلِهِ** — **When Moses said to his family** (27:7) etc. What is meant by "his family" (أهله) is his wife, who was the daughter of Shu‘ayb, according to what Allah, the Exalted, mentioned in Surah al-Qasas. It is mentioned in *al-Majma‘*: "The address to her in His saying: **آتِيكُمْ** (I will bring you) in the plural form is because she stands in the place of a group in providing companionship (الأنس) in desolate places." End quote. It is also possible that there were others with her, such as a servant, a cameleer, or someone else.

In *al-Majma‘*: "Perceiving (الإيناس) means seeing (الإبصار)." It is also said: "You perceived (آنست) something when you sensed it from a direction that provides comfort (يؤنس بها); whatever you find comfort in, you have sensed it while your soul is at peace with it." End quote. A brand (الشهاب), according to *al-Majma‘*, is a light like a pillar of fire; every light that extends like a pillar is called a *shihab*, and what is meant here is a flame of fire. In *al-Mufradat*: "The brand (الشهاب) is the bright flame of a lit fire or a meteor in the atmosphere." In *al-Mufradat* as well: "The firebrand (القبس) is that which is taken from a flame, and warming oneself (الاصطلاء) by the fire means seeking heat from it."

The context of the verse—supported by what occurs of the story in other Surahs—testifies that he was traveling with his family at that time, had lost the way, and he and his family were struck by the cold on a dark night. He saw a fire from afar and intended to go to it; if he found a person there, he would ask for information, or he would take a firebrand to bring to his family so they could light a fire to warm themselves. Thus, he said to his family: "Stay here, for I have sensed and seen a fire; remain in your place. I will bring you from it—meaning from its location—news by which we may be guided, or I will bring you a flame taken from the fire so that you may light a fire to warm and heat yourselves."

It also appears from the context that the fire only appeared to him (peace be upon him) and was not seen by anyone else; otherwise, it would have been referred to with a demonstrative pronoun rather than an indefinite noun.

Perhaps the difference in nature between bringing news and bringing fire is what necessitated the repetition of the word "bring" (الإتيان), where He said: **سَآتِيكُمْ مِنْها بِخَبَرٍ أَوْ آتِيكُمْ بِشِهابٍ قَبَسٍ** — **I will bring you some news from it, or I will bring you a firebrand** (27:7).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلَمَّا جاءَها نُودِيَ أَنْ بُورِكَ مَنْ فِي النَّارِ وَمَنْ حَوْلَها وَسُبْحانَ اللهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** — **When he came to it, he was called: ‘Blessed is He who is in the fire and whoever is around it! Immaculate is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds!’** (27:8). Meaning: when he reached the fire and was present at its location, he was called: "Blessed is..." etc.

What is meant by blessing (المباركة) is the bestowing of abundant good. It is said: "He blessed him" (باركه), "blessed him" (بارك عليه), and "blessed in him" (بارك فيه), meaning He clothed him in abundant good and endowed him with it. In Surah Taha, at this point in the story, His saying occurs: **فَلَمَّا أَتاها نُودِيَ يا مُوسى إِنِّي أَنَا رَبُّكَ فَاخْلَعْ نَعْلَيْكَ إِنَّكَ بِالْوادِ الْمُقَدَّسِ طُوىً وَأَنَا اخْتَرْتُكَ فَاسْتَمِعْ لِما يُوحى** — **When he came to it, he was called, ‘O Moses! Indeed I am your Lord! So take off your sandals. You are indeed in the sacred valley of Tuwa. I have chosen you; so listen to what is revealed.** (20:11-13). It can be inferred from this that what is meant by "whoever is around the fire" is Musa, or that he is among those around the fire, and his being blessed is his being chosen after his sanctification.

As for what is meant by "whoever is in the fire," it has been said: It means He whose authority and power appeared in the fire, for the speaking was from the tree—as mentioned in Surah al-Qasas—and the fire had surrounded it. Based on this, the meaning is: Blessed is He who manifested Himself to you through His speech from the fire, and He has blessed you. His saying: **وَسُبْحانَ اللهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** — **Immaculate is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds!** (27:8) would then be a glorification of Him, the Glorified, from being a body (جسماً) or corporeal (جسمانياً) such that space could encompass Him or accidents (الحدثان) could be adjacent to Him; it is not to express Musa's wonder, as has been suggested.

It has also been said: What is meant by "whoever is in the fire" are the angels present within it, just as what is meant by "whoever is around it" is Musa (peace be upon him).

It has also been said: What is meant by it is Musa (peace be upon him), and by "whoever is around it" are the angels.

It has also been said: There is an ellipsis in the speech, and the original meaning is: Blessed is whoever is in the place where the fire is—which is the blessed spot (البقعة المباركة) in which the tree was, as in Surah al-Qasas—and the one in it is Musa; and "around it" refers to the Holy Land (الأرض المقدسة) which is the Levant (الشامات), and "those around it" are the prophets residing therein from the progeny of Ibrahim and the Children of Israel.

It has also been said: What is meant by "whoever is in the fire" is the light of Allah, the Exalted, and by "whoever is around it" is Musa.

It has also been said: What is meant by "whoever is in the fire" is the tree, for it was surrounded by the fire, and by "whoever is around it" are the angels who glorify Him.

Most of these views are not free from obvious arbitrariness (تحكم).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا مُوسى إِنَّهُ أَنَا اللهُ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** — **‘O Moses! Indeed I am Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise.’** (27:9). This is a self-introduction from Him, the Exalted, to Musa (peace be upon him) so that he may know that the One addressing him with speech is his Lord, the Exalted. Thus, this verse in this Surah corresponds to His saying in Surah Taha: **نُودِيَ يا مُوسى إِنِّي أَنَا رَبُّكَ فَاخْلَعْ** — **he was called, ‘O Moses! Indeed I am your Lord! So take off...** (20:11-12). So refer to Surah Taha and contemplate the verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَلْقِ عَصاكَ فَلَمَّا رَآها تَهْتَزُّ كَأَنَّها جَانٌّ وَلَّى مُدْبِراً وَلَمْ يُعَقِّبْ** — **‘Throw down your staff!’ And when he saw it writhing as if it were a snake, he turned his back [to flee], and did not look back.** (27:10) etc. Writhing (الاهتزاز) is intense movement. The *jann* (الجان) is a small, fast-moving snake. Turning one's back (الإدبار) is the opposite of facing forward. Looking back (التعقيب) is returning after fleeing; it is said of a fighter when he returns to the fray after his flight.

In the verse, there is an omission and brevity (إيجاز) indicated by the "explanatory *fa*" (الفاء الفصيحة) in His saying: **فَلَمَّا رَآها تَهْتَزُّ** — **And when he saw it writhing**. The implied meaning is: "And throw down your staff; so when he threw it down, behold, it was a manifest serpent (ثعبان مبين) writhing as if it were a *jann*; and when he saw it writhing..." etc.

There is no contradiction between the staff becoming a manifest serpent (ثعبان مبين)—as occurs in his story (peace be upon him) in Surahs al-A‘raf and al-Shu‘ara’, where the serpent (الثعبان) is a snake of massive body—and its being likened in this Surah to a *jann*. This is because the comparison only concerns the writhing, the speed of movement, and the agitation. He witnessed the staff having transformed into a serpent of massive body and terrifying appearance, yet it was writhing and moving with the speed of a *jann*'s writhing and movement. It is not a comparison of the staff or the serpent itself to the *jann* itself.

It has been said: The sign of the staff appeared differently; the staff appeared the first time in the form of a *jann*, as occurs in Surah Taha: **فَأَلْقاها فَإِذا هِيَ حَيَّةٌ تَسْعى** — **So he threw it down, and behold, it was a snake, running.** (20:20). Then it appeared, when he threw it before Pharaoh, in the form of a manifest serpent (ثعبان مبين), as in Surahs al-A‘raf and al-Shu‘ara’.

The issue with this view is that, although it is not without merit considering the context of the verses, it does not resolve the problem of a thing being compared to itself or its not having transformed into a snake. Thus, reliance in resolving the problem should be placed on what was previously mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا مُوسى لا تَخَفْ إِنِّي لا يَخافُ لَدَيَّ الْمُرْسَلُونَ** — **‘O Moses! Do not be afraid. Indeed the apostles are not afraid in My presence,** (27:10). This is a narration of the actual address issued there, and it carries the meaning: "Allah said: O Musa, do not be afraid..." etc.

His saying: **لا تَخَفْ** — **Do not be afraid** is an absolute prohibition that grants him security from everything harmful that might be feared as long as he is in the presence of proximity (حضرة القرب) and direct address, whether the object of fear is the staff or something else. For this reason, the prohibition was justified by His saying: **إِنِّي لا يَخافُ لَدَيَّ الْمُرْسَلُونَ** — **Indeed the apostles are not afraid in My presence**. The qualification of the negation with His saying: **لَدَيَ** — **in My presence** indicates that the station of proximity and presence necessitates security (الأمن) and is incompatible with any disliked thing that is feared. This is supported by the replacement of this sentence in the story in Surah al-Qasas with His saying: **إِنَّكَ مِنَ الْآمِنِينَ** — **you are [one] of those who are secure** (28:31). Thus, the meaning becomes: Do not fear anything; you are an apostle, and the apostles—who are in My presence in the station of proximity—are in a station of security, and there is no fear alongside security.

As for Musa's (peace be upon him) flight from the staff when it took that terrifying form, writhing as if it were a *jann*, it was an act in accordance with what Allah has ingrained in human nature (الطبيعة الإنسانية) when suddenly confronted by dangers from which there is no way to protect oneself except by flight. He was unarmed, having no weapon with him except his staff, and it was that very staff he now feared for himself. No prior command had come to him from Allah, the Exalted, to remain in his place, nor any prohibition against fleeing from what he feared for himself, except for His saying: **وَأَلْقِ عَصاكَ** — **Throw down your staff!**, which he had obeyed. Fleeing from great dangers that cannot be repelled except by flight is not part of the blameworthy cowardice for which one would be censured.

Furthermore, the fact that the prophets and apostles do not fear anything while they are with their Lord—as indicated by His saying: **إِنِّي لا يَخافُ لَدَيَّ الْمُرْسَلُونَ** — is not a dignity (كرامة) they possess of their own accord. Rather, it is through instruction (تعليم) and refinement (تأديب) from Allah. Since the occasion at the night of Mount Tur was the first occasion in which Allah brought Musa near to Him, singled him out for speech, and endowed him with messengership and dignity, His saying: **لا تَخَفْ إِنَّكَ مِنَ الْآمِنِينَ** — **Do not be afraid; you are [one] of those who are secure** and His saying: **لا تَخَفْ إِنِّي لا يَخافُ لَدَيَّ الْمُرْسَلُونَ** — **Do not be afraid. Indeed the apostles are not afraid in My presence** constitute divine instruction and refinement for him (peace be upon him).

It thus becomes clear that His saying: **لا تَخَفْ إِنِّي لا يَخافُ لَدَيَّ الْمُرْسَلُونَ** is a divine refinement and upbringing (تربية) for Musa (peace be upon him), and is not in any way a form of rebuke or reprimand.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِلَّا مَنْ ظَلَمَ ثُمَّ بَدَّلَ حُسْناً بَعْدَ سُوءٍ فَإِنِّي غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — **excepting him who has done wrong, then substituted good for evil. Indeed I am all-forgiving, all-merciful.** (27:11). What ought to be said—and Allah knows best—is that when the previous verse informed that the apostles are secure and do not fear, it was understood from it that others among the people of wrongdoing (أهل الظلم) are not secure and have reason to fear. Thus, this verse makes an exception for the state of the people of repentance (أهل التوبة) from among the people of wrongdoing. It clarifies that because of their repentance and their substituting their wrongdoing—which is the evil (السوء)—with good after the evil, they are forgiven and shown mercy; thus, they also do not fear.

The exception is from the apostles, and it is a "disjunctive exception" (استثناء منقطع). What is meant by wrongdoing (الظلم) is absolute disobedience (المعصية), and by "good after evil" (الحسن بعد السوء) is repentance after disobedience or righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) after an evil one. The meaning is: But whoever does wrong by committing disobedience, then substitutes that with good after evil—meaning repentance after disobedience or a righteous deed after an evil one—then indeed I am All-Forgiving, All-Merciful; I forgive his wrongdoing and show him mercy, so let him not fear anything after that.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَدْخِلْ يَدَكَ فِي جَيْبِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضاءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوءٍ** — **‘Insert your hand into your shirt room: it will emerge white, without any ill,** (27:12) etc. "Ill" (السوء) has been interpreted as leprosy, as previously mentioned. His saying: **فِي تِسْعِ آياتٍ إِلى فِرْعَوْنَ وَقَوْمِهِ** — **among nine signs to Pharaoh and his people.** (27:12). It can be inferred from the context, firstly, that "among nine" (فِي تِسْعِ) is a "circumstantial qualifier" (حال) for both signs together. The meaning is: I have given you these two signs—the staff and the hand—while they are among nine signs.

Secondly: that the two signs are among the nine signs. A discussion detailing the nine signs has already passed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسى تِسْعَ آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ** — **Certainly We gave Moses nine manifest signs.** (17:101). The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلَمَّا جاءَتْهُمْ آياتُنا مُبْصِرَةً قالُوا هذا سِحْرٌ مُبِينٌ** — **But when Our signs came to them visibly, they said, ‘This is plain magic.’** (27:13). "Visibly" (مبصرة) means clear and manifest. In their saying: **هذا سِحْرٌ مُبِينٌ** — **This is plain magic** there is a belittling and insult toward the signs, as they ignored the significance of the signs' specificities, even their number, paying no heed to them except as some "thing."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَجَحَدُوا بِها وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْها أَنْفُسُهُمْ ظُلْماً وَعُلُوًّا** — **They impugned them—though they were convinced of them in their hearts—out of wrong and arrogance.** (27:14) etc. Al-Raghib said: "Denial (الجحد) is to negate what is affirmed in the heart, or to affirm what is negated in the heart." End quote. Conviction (الاستيقان) and certainty (الإيقان) carry the same meaning.
